01 —

About Me

I'm a resourceful and dynamic data professional with a Bachelor of Science in Economics and Statistics (Second Class – Upper Division) from the University of Nairobi, advancing through Moringa School's intensive Data Science programme.

My analytical instinct was forged through rigorous training in macroeconomic modelling, statistical inference, and quantitative research. I combine that foundation with hands-on expertise in Python, machine learning, and data visualisation to deliver insights that directly inform business decisions.

I currently intern at the Kenya Revenue Authority, analysing revenue data, preparing dashboards, and collaborating with teams to reconcile tax data. I'm also pursuing ACCA qualifications at KCA University, reinforcing financial acumen alongside technical skills.

03 —

Projects

01

Aviation Safety Risk Analysis

NTSB Historical Accident Data 1962–2023  ·  Phase 1 Data Science Project  ·  Moringa School

PythonPandasMatplotlibSeabornEDAData CleaningBusiness Intelligence
+

Business Problem

A company diversifying into commercial aviation lacked domain expertise on risk associated with different aircraft models. The stakeholder — Head of the New Aviation Division — needed a data-driven answer to: which aircraft are safest to buy?

The analysis used NTSB accident records spanning over 60 years (1962–2023), filtered to commercial operations only, to surface survivability rates, manufacturer reliability, and operational resilience.

Data Cleaning Steps

  • Removed write-offs — excluded total hull losses, keeping only survivable incidents
  • Excluded private operators — filtered to commercial/professional flights only
  • Dropped missing registrations and standardised date formats
  • Created two subsets — Substantial Damage (safety) and Minor/No Damage (resilience)
  • Extracted manufacturer from aircraft type string via Python parsing

Key Findings

  • Cessna 208B Grand Caravan led survivability with 55+ substantial-damage accidents and zero fatalities
  • Boeing 737-8AS (WL) topped operational resilience with 18 minor/no-damage incidents — highest structural durability
  • Embraer was the safest manufacturer — 58 zero-fatality incidents, far ahead of all competitors
  • Risk trend shows declining incident rates over the last decade — improving safety environment
  • Airbus A330-243, A320-232, and A320-214 also ranked highly for resilience

MetricTop PerformerValue
SurvivabilityCessna 208B Grand Caravan55+ survived, 0 deaths
ResilienceBoeing 737-8AS (WL)18 minor incidents
ManufacturerEmbraer58 zero-fatality events
TrendIndustry-wide↓ Declining risk
📓 View Full Notebook on GitHub →

Four visualisations were produced to answer the core business questions. Click any chart to zoom in.

Chart 1 — Survivability: Aircraft with Zero Fatalities
Chart 2 — Operational Resilience: Minor / No Damage Leaders
Chart 3 — Manufacturer Reliability: Safest Brands
Chart 4 — Risk Trend: Last 10 Years
Recommendation 1 — Prioritise High Survivability Models

Acquire the Cessna 208B Grand Caravan as the primary fleet choice. Its proven record of zero fatalities across 55+ substantial-damage events minimises passenger liability, insurance costs, and reputational risk for a new operator entering the market.

Recommendation 2 — Invest in Operationally Resilient Aircraft

Boeing 737-8AS (WL), Airbus A330-243, and Airbus A320-232 demonstrate the highest structural durability, surviving incidents with only minor damage. This reduces total-loss risk and costly hull write-offs — critical for protecting capital in the early years of fleet operation.

Recommendation 3 — Partner with Proven Manufacturers

Limit initial acquisitions to Embraer, Shorts, and Raytheon — the manufacturers with the lowest average fatality rates per incident. Aligning with safety-focused engineering partners reduces systemic fleet risk and signals commitment to safety standards to regulators and insurers.

02

Box Office Intelligence — Movie Studio EDA

5 Datasets · 3,359 Films · 2010–2018 · Group 1, Moringa School Data Science Cohort

PythonPandasSeabornSciPyMulti-Dataset MergeROI AnalysisEDA
+

Business Problem

A major company planned to launch a new movie studio but had no experience in film production and no data-driven greenlight strategy. With high production costs and uncertain returns, the team needed to know: which genres, budgets, and quality levels deliver the best ROI?

The analysis merged five industry datasets covering 3,359+ films released 2010–2018 from Box Office Mojo, The Numbers, TMDB, and Rotten Tomatoes, engineering profitability and ROI metrics to surface actionable strategy.

Data Sources

SourceCoverageKey Metric
Box Office Mojo3,359 filmsDomestic & foreign gross
The Numbers5,415 filmsProduction budgets, ROI
TMDBGenre dataGenre tags per film
Rotten Tomatoes54,432 reviewsFresh % per film

Key Findings

  • Sci-Fi & Fantasy averaged $81M per film — #1 earning genre with a strong median
  • Action & Adventure offered the best volume-performance balance at $62M avg across 366 films
  • Animation had near-zero failure rate — almost no wide-release animated films lost money
  • Films at $100M–$200M budget achieved a 93.1% profit rate — the clear sweet spot
  • Certified Fresh films earned +30% more than Rotten titles ($47M vs $36M avg)
  • PG-13 maximised audience reach at $57M avg vs $23M for R-rated
  • Market grew 26% over the period — fewer but bigger films each year
  • Disney earned 2× nearest rival per film ($417M vs $230M avg)
📓 View Full Notebook on GitHub →

All visualisations are from the actual presentation delivered to stakeholders. Click any chart to view full size.

Analysis 1 — Genre Revenue: Average & Median Box Office per Film
Finding 1 — Genre Revenue Summary
Analysis 2 — Budget & ROI: Profit Rate by Budget Tier
Finding 2 — Budget & ROI Summary
Analysis 3 — Critical Reception: Rotten Tomatoes Score vs Box Office
Bonus — Market Context: Total Gross Trend, Studio Performance, MPAA Rating, Runtime
Final Recommendations Summary Slide
Recommendation 1 — Lead with Sci-Fi, Action & Animation

These genres average $56M–$81M per film. Target PG-13 for maximum audience breadth ($57M avg vs $23M for R-rated). Avoid Drama ($28M) and Documentary ($12M) as lead genres. Use Horror micro-budget ($1–5M) as a secondary cash-flow diversification play.

Recommendation 2 — Budget $100M–$200M per Tent-Pole

This tier achieves a 93.1% profit rate with 2.79× median ROI — a $150M film earns ~$418M worldwide at median. Avoid the $10M–$50M range (66% profit rate, worst risk-adjusted tier). Scale to $200M+ as the studio infrastructure matures.

Recommendation 3 — Invest in Quality — Target Certified Fresh

Certified Fresh films (RT ≥ 91%) earn 30% more revenue. Hire directors with strong critical track records for the first three films. Budget $2M–$5M for script development before greenlighting. Delay release rather than cut quality — date flexibility is a competitive advantage.
